"*Qui Pro Domina Justitia Sequitur*" ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"): "Who prosecutes on behalf of justice (or the [Lady Justice](/wiki/Lady_Justice "Lady Justice"))")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Madan08-2)

* [Pam Bondi](/wiki/Pam_Bondi "Pam Bondi"), [Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") * [Todd Blanche](/wiki/Todd_Blanche "Todd Blanche"), [Deputy Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Deputy_Attorney_General "United States Deputy Attorney General") * [Stanley Woodward Jr.](/wiki/Stanley_Woodward_Jr. "Stanley Woodward Jr."), [Associate Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Associate_Attorney_General "United States Associate Attorney General") * [D. John Sauer](/wiki/D._John_Sauer "D. John Sauer"), [Solicitor General](/wiki/Solicitor_General_of_the_United_States "Solicitor General of the United States")

(**DOJ**) is an [executive department](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [United States federal government](/wiki/United_States_federal_government "United States federal government") that oversees the domestic enforcement of [federal laws](/wiki/Law_of_the_United_States "Law of the United States") and the [administration of justice](/wiki/Administration_of_justice "Administration of justice"). It is equivalent to the [justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_justice "Ministry of justice") or [interior ministries](/wiki/Interior_ministries "Interior ministries") of other countries. The department is headed by the [United States attorney general](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General"), who reports directly to the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and is a member of the president's [Cabinet](/wiki/United_States_Cabinet "United States Cabinet"). [Pam Bondi](/wiki/Pam_Bondi "Pam Bondi") has served as U.S. attorney general since February 2025.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The **United States Department of Justice** (**DOJ**) is an [executive department](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [United States federal government](/wiki/United_States_federal_government "United States federal government") that oversees the domestic enforcement of [federal laws](/wiki/Law_of_the_United_States "Law of the United States") and the [administration of justice](/wiki/Administration_of_justice "Administration of justice"). It is equivalent to the [justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_justice "Ministry of justice") or [interior ministries](/wiki/Interior_ministries "Interior ministries") of other countries. The department is headed by the [United States attorney general](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General"), who reports directly to the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and is a member of the president's [Cabinet](/wiki/United_States_Cabinet "United States Cabinet"). [Pam Bondi](/wiki/Pam_Bondi "Pam Bondi") has served as U.S. attorney general since February 2025.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Justice Department contains most of the United States' [federal law enforcement agencies](/wiki/Federal_law_enforcement_in_the_United_States "Federal law enforcement in the United States"), including the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"), the [U.S. Marshals Service](/wiki/U.S._Marshals_Service "U.S. Marshals Service"), the [Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives](/wiki/Bureau_of_Alcohol,_Tobacco,_Firearms_and_Explosives "Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives"), the [Drug Enforcement Administration](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration"), and the [Federal Bureau of Prisons](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Prisons "Federal Bureau of Prisons"). The department also has eight divisions of lawyers who represent the federal government in litigation: the [Criminal](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Criminal_Division "United States Department of Justice Criminal Division"), [Civil](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Civil_Division "United States Department of Justice Civil Division"), [Antitrust](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Antitrust_Division "United States Department of Justice Antitrust Division"), [Tax](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Tax_Division "United States Department of Justice Tax Division"), [Civil Rights](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Civil_Rights_Division "United States Department of Justice Civil Rights Division"), [Environment and Natural Resources](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Environment_and_Natural_Resources_Division "United States Department of Justice Environment and Natural Resources Division"), [National Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_National_Security_Division "United States Department of Justice National Security Division"), and [Justice Management](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Justice_Management_Division "United States Department of Justice Justice Management Division") divisions. The DOJ includes the [United States attorneys](/wiki/United_States_Attorney "United States Attorney")' offices for each of the 94 [United States federal judicial districts](/wiki/United_States_federal_judicial_district "United States federal judicial district").

The [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") created the Justice Department in 1870, during the [presidency of Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ulysses_S._Grant "Presidency of Ulysses S. Grant"). Its functions originally date to 1789, when Congress created the office of the Attorney General. The department is headquartered in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") at the [Robert F. Kennedy Department of Justice Building](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Department_of_Justice_Building "Robert F. Kennedy Department of Justice Building").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Justice&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The office of the [attorney general](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") was established by the [Judiciary Act of 1789](/wiki/Judiciary_Act_of_1789 "Judiciary Act of 1789") as a part-time job for one person, but grew with the [bureaucracy](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy"). At one time, the attorney general gave legal advice to the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress"), as well as the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"); however, in 1819, the attorney general's role as legal counsel for Congress ended as the workload of the office increased.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Until 1853, the salary of the attorney general was set by statute at less than the amount paid to other Cabinet members. Early attorneys general supplemented their salaries by running private law practices, often arguing cases before the courts as attorneys for paying litigants.[[2]](#cite_note-Madan08-2) The lightness of the office is exemplified by [Edward Bates](/wiki/Edward_Bates "Edward Bates") (1793–1869), attorney general under President Abraham Lincoln (1861–1864). Bates had only a small operation, with a staff of just six. Their main function was to generate legal opinions at the request of Lincoln and cabinet members and to handle occasional cases before the Supreme Court. However, Lincoln's cabinet was full of experienced lawyers who seldom felt the need to ask for his opinions. In addition, Bates had no authority over U.S. Attorneys across the country. The federal court system was handled by the [Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior"), and the [Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") handled claims. Most of the opinions turned out by Bates's office were of minor importance. Lincoln gave him no special assignments and did not seek his advice on Supreme Court appointments. Bates did have an opportunity to comment on general policy as a cabinet member with a strong political base, but he seldom spoke up.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Following unsuccessful efforts in 1830 and 1846 to make the attorney general a full-time position,[[2]](#cite_note-Madan08-2) in 1867 the [United States House Committee on the Judiciary](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_the_Judiciary "United States House Committee on the Judiciary"), led by Congressman [William Lawrence](/wiki/William_Lawrence_(Ohio) "William Lawrence (Ohio)"), conducted an inquiry into the creation of a "law department" headed by the attorney general and composed of the various department solicitors and [United States attorneys](/wiki/United_States_attorney "United States attorney"). On February 19, 1868, Lawrence introduced a bill in Congress to create the Department of Justice. President [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") signed the bill into law on June 22, 1870.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The "Act to Establish the Department of Justice" drastically increased the attorney general's responsibilities to include the supervision of all United States attorneys, formerly under the Department of the Interior, the prosecution of all federal crimes, and the representation of the United States in all court actions, barring the use of private attorneys by the federal government.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The law also created the office of [Solicitor General](/wiki/United_States_Solicitor_General "United States Solicitor General") to supervise and conduct government litigation in the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The organization of the department was a part of the general effort to control [patronage](/wiki/Spoils_system "Spoils system"), [retrenchment](/wiki/Retrenchment "Retrenchment") in the workforce, and to improve the status of lawyers.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Grant appointed [Amos T. Akerman](/wiki/Amos_T._Akerman "Amos T. Akerman") as attorney general and [Benjamin H. Bristow](/wiki/Benjamin_H._Bristow "Benjamin H. Bristow") as America's first solicitor general the same week that Congress created the Department of Justice. The Department's immediate function was to preserve civil rights. It set about fighting against domestic terrorist groups who had been using both violence and litigation to oppose the [13th](/wiki/Thirteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), [14th](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), and [15th Amendments](/wiki/Fifteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") to the Constitution.[[11]](#cite_note-chernow-p700-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Thomas_Nast_1874.jpg/250px-Thomas_Nast_1874.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Nast_1874.jpg)

[Thomas Nast](/wiki/Thomas_Nast "Thomas Nast") illustration entitled "Halt," published October 17, 1874

Both Akerman and Bristow used the Department of Justice to vigorously prosecute [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") members in the early 1870s. In the first few years of Grant's first term in office, there were 1000 indictments against Klan members, with over 550 convictions from the Department of Justice. By 1871, there were 3000 indictments and 600 convictions, with most only serving brief sentences, while the ringleaders were imprisoned for up to five years in the federal penitentiary in [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"). The result was a dramatic decrease in violence in the South. Akerman gave credit to Grant and told a friend that no one was "better" or "stronger" than Grant when it came to prosecuting terrorists.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [George H. Williams](/wiki/George_Henry_Williams "George Henry Williams"), who succeeded Akerman in December 1871, continued to prosecute the Klan throughout 1872 until the spring of 1873, during Grant's second term in office. Williams then placed a [moratorium](/wiki/Moratorium_(law) "Moratorium (law)") on Klan prosecutions partially because the Justice Department, inundated by cases involving the Klan, did not have the manpower to continue prosecutions.[[13]](#cite_note-Willams_p123-13)

With the passage of the [Interstate Commerce Act](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Act "Interstate Commerce Act") in 1887, the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") took on some law enforcement responsibilities, and the Department of Justice was tasked with performing these.[[14]](#cite_note-langeluttig-p9-14)

In 1884, control of federal prisons was transferred to the new department, from the Department of the Interior. New facilities were built, including the penitentiary at [Leavenworth](/wiki/United_States_Penitentiary,_Leavenworth "United States Penitentiary, Leavenworth") in 1895, and a facility for women located in [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia"), at [Alderson](/wiki/Alderson_Federal_Prison_Camp "Alderson Federal Prison Camp") was established in 1924.[[15]](#cite_note-langeluttig-p14-15)

In 1933, President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") issued an executive order which gave the Department of Justice responsibility for the "functions of prosecuting in the courts of the United States claims and demands by, and offsenses [*sic*] against, the Government of the United States, and of defending claims and demands against the Government, and of supervising the work of United States attorneys, marshals, and clerks in connection therewith, now exercised by any agency or officer..."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Following the [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal"), reforms were enacted to enhance the independence of the Justice Department including the [Ethics in Government Act](/wiki/Ethics_in_Government_Act "Ethics in Government Act"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) and for the next 50 years, subsequent Attorneys General reinforced the independence of the Department of Justice from the president.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![President Trump's face on the US Department of Justice's headquarters](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Donald_Trump_banner_on_DOJ_building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._%282026-02-26%29.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_banner_on_DOJ_building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._%282026-02-26%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_banner_on_DOJ_building,_Washington,_D.C._(2026-02-26).jpg)

President Trump's face on the US Department of Justice's headquarters

Further information: [Democratic backsliding in the United States](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States "Democratic backsliding in the United States")

During the [second Trump presidency](/wiki/Second_Trump_presidency "Second Trump presidency"), the Department's "independence and impartiality has shattered".[[19]](#cite_note-NYT_Nov_17_2025-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) The department has been used [to target](/wiki/Targeting_of_political_opponents_and_civil_society_under_the_second_Trump_administration "Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration") Trump's political opponents. Trump reportedly demanded $230 million in compensation from the DOJ and the department ended an investigation into [Tom Homan](/wiki/Tom_Homan "Tom Homan") receiving $50,000 which have raised [ethical concerns](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump#Ethics "Second presidency of Donald Trump").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) As part of [Republican Party efforts to disrupt voting after the 2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/Republican_Party_efforts_to_disrupt_voting_after_the_2024_United_States_presidential_election "Republican Party efforts to disrupt voting after the 2024 United States presidential election"), the DOJ sought voter roll data from over 30 states and initiated the [2025 Texas redistricting](/wiki/2025_Texas_redistricting "2025 Texas redistricting").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Justice&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

Main article: [Robert F. Kennedy Department of Justice Building](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Department_of_Justice_Building "Robert F. Kennedy Department of Justice Building")

The U.S. Department of Justice Building was completed in 1935 to a design by architect [Milton Bennett Medary](/wiki/Milton_Bennett_Medary "Milton Bennett Medary"). After Medary's death in 1929, the other partners of his Philadelphia firm [Zantzinger, Borie and Medary](/wiki/Zantzinger,_Borie_and_Medary "Zantzinger, Borie and Medary") completed the project. The building occupies a site bounded by Constitution and Pennsylvania Avenues and Ninth and Tenth Streets, Northwest, in Washington, D.C., and contains more than 1,000,000 sq ft (93,000 m2) of interior space.

The Latin motto on the Department of Justice seal, *Qui Pro Domina Justitia Sequitur*, has been the subject of various interpretations, none of which has been confirmed as the original intended meaning. It is not known exactly when the original version of the DOJ seal was adopted or when the motto first appeared on it. The most authoritative view from the Department of Justice itself is that the motto refers to the Attorney General—and by extension the Department—as one who "prosecutes on behalf of justice (or the Lady Justice)."[[2]](#cite_note-Madan08-2)

This concept of the prosecutor as a servant of justice is reflected in a similar English inscription carved in the paneling above the doorway in the ceremonial rotunda anteroom just outside the Attorney General's office: "THE UNITED STATES WINS ITS POINT WHENEVER JUSTICE IS DONE ITS CITIZENS IN THE COURTS."[[2]](#cite_note-Madan08-2)

The building was renamed the Robert F. Kennedy Department of Justice Building in 2001 in honor of former Attorney General [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"). It is sometimes called "Main Justice."[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Law enforcement agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Justice&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Law enforcement agencies")]

Several [federal law enforcement agencies](/wiki/Federal_law_enforcement_in_the_United_States "Federal law enforcement in the United States") are administered by the Department of Justice:

* [United States Marshals Service](/wiki/United_States_Marshals_Service "United States Marshals Service") (USMS) – The office of U.S. Marshal was established by the [Judiciary Act of 1789](/wiki/Judiciary_Act_of_1789 "Judiciary Act of 1789"). The U.S. Marshals Service was established as an agency in 1969, and it was elevated to full bureau status under the Justice Department in 1974.[[36]](#cite_note-39)[[37]](#cite_note-40)
* [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") (FBI) – On July 26, 1908, a small investigative force was created within the Justice Department under Attorney General [Charles Bonaparte](/wiki/Charles_Bonaparte_(Attorney_General) "Charles Bonaparte (Attorney General)"). The following year, this force was officially named the Bureau of Investigation by Attorney General [George W. Wickersham](/wiki/George_W._Wickersham "George W. Wickersham"). In 1935, the bureau adopted its current name.[[38]](#cite_note-41)
* [Federal Bureau of Prisons](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Prisons "Federal Bureau of Prisons") (BOP) – the Three Prisons Act of 1891 created the federal prison system. Congress created the Federal Bureau of Prisons in 1930 by Pub. L. No. 71–218, 46 Stat. 325, signed into law by President Hoover on May 14, 1930.[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44)
  + [National Institute of Corrections](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Corrections "National Institute of Corrections") (NIC) – Founded in 1974, the National Institute of Corrections is organized under the [Federal Bureau of Prisons](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Prisons "Federal Bureau of Prisons") and has a legislatively mandated mission to assist state and local correctional institutions, and to manage the [American Federal Prison System](/wiki/Incarceration_in_the_United_States "Incarceration in the United States") by keeping records of inmates.
* [Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives](/wiki/Bureau_of_Alcohol,_Tobacco,_Firearms_and_Explosives "Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives") (ATF) – Except for a brief period during [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_era "Prohibition era"), ATF's predecessor bureaus were part of the Department of the Treasury for more than two hundred years. ATF was first established by Department of Treasury Order No. 221, effective July 1, 1972; this order transferred the functions, powers, and duties arising under laws relating to alcohol, tobacco, firearms, and explosives from the Internal Revenue Service to ATF.[[42]](#cite_note-45) In 2003, under the terms of the [Homeland Security Act](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Act "Homeland Security Act"), ATF was split into two agencies – the new Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) was transferred to the Department of Justice, while the [Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau](/wiki/Alcohol_and_Tobacco_Tax_and_Trade_Bureau "Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau") (TTB) was retained by the Department of the Treasury.[[43]](#cite_note-46)
* [Drug Enforcement Administration](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration") (DEA) – Created in 1973 as part of the [war on drugs](/wiki/War_on_drugs "War on drugs"), the DEA was formed from various previously existing law enforcement agencies that were parts of either the Department of Justice, [Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") or the [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration"). The DEA enforces the [Controlled Substances Act](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act "Controlled Substances Act") and also interdicts foreign drug trafficking.
* [Office of the Inspector General](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice_Office_of_the_Inspector_General "United States Department of Justice Office of the Inspector General") (OIG) – The Office of Inspector General performs basic internal auditing functions, and has the power to make arrests and prosecute members of the Department of Justice who are found to be in violation of laws regulating conduct of government officials.

### Other offices and programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Justice&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other offices and programs")]

* [Foreign Claims Settlement Commission of the United States](/wiki/Foreign_Claims_Settlement_Commission_of_the_United_States "Foreign Claims Settlement Commission of the United States")
* [INTERPOL](/wiki/INTERPOL "INTERPOL"), U.S. National Central Bureau
* [Obscenity Prosecution Task Force](/wiki/Obscenity_Prosecution_Task_Force "Obscenity Prosecution Task Force")
* [United States Parole Commission](/wiki/United_States_Parole_Commission "United States Parole Commission")

In March 2003, the [United States Immigration and Naturalization Service](/wiki/United_States_Immigration_and_Naturalization_Service "United States Immigration and Naturalization Service") was abolished and its functions transferred to the [United States Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security"). The [Executive Office for Immigration Review](/wiki/Executive_Office_for_Immigration_Review "Executive Office for Immigration Review") and the [Board of Immigration Appeals](/wiki/Board_of_Immigration_Appeals "Board of Immigration Appeals"), which review decisions made by government officials under Immigration and Nationality law, remain under jurisdiction of the Department of Justice. Similarly the Office of Domestic Preparedness left the Justice Department for the Department of Homeland Security, but only for executive purposes. The Office of Domestic Preparedness is still centralized within the Department of Justice, since its personnel are still officially employed within the Department of Justice.

In 2003, the Department of Justice created *LifeAndLiberty.gov*, a website defending the [USA PATRIOT Act](/wiki/USA_PATRIOT_Act "USA PATRIOT Act"), as part of Attorney General [John Ashcroft](/wiki/John_Ashcroft "John Ashcroft")'s public campaign to bolster support for the law.[[46]](#cite_note-49) It was criticized by government watchdog groups for its alleged violation of U.S. Code Title 18 Section 1913, which forbids money appropriated by Congress to be used to lobby in favor of any law, actual or proposed.[[47]](#cite_note-dotgovwatch-50) The website has since been taken offline.

On October 6, 2021, U.S. Deputy Attorney General Lisa Monaco announced the creation of the National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Team (NCET).[[48]](#cite_note-51)
